label: Strong clinical summary with specific outcomes and treatment protocols
text:
Clinical Summary: Multidisciplinary Management of Treatment-Resistant Type 2 Diabetes

Patient Profile
A 58-year-old male (BMI 34.2 kg/m2) with a 12-year history of Type 2 diabetes mellitus, referred to the specialist metabolic clinic following persistent suboptimal glycaemic control despite sequential escalation of oral hypoglycaemic agents. At referral, HbA1c was 78 mmol/mol (9.3%), having remained above 69 mmol/mol on three consecutive quarterly measurements. Comorbidities included stage 3a CKD (eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73m2), hypertension (controlled on amlodipine 10mg and ramipril 10mg), and background diabetic retinopathy confirmed on annual screening.

Treatment Protocol
Following multidisciplinary review (diabetologist, diabetes specialist nurse, dietitian, psychologist), the following changes were implemented at week 0: (1) initiation of semaglutide 0.25mg weekly, titrated to 1.0mg over 8 weeks per NICE NG28 guidance; (2) discontinuation of gliclazide 80mg BD due to hypoglycaemia risk with GLP-1RA combination; (3) continuation of metformin 1g BD (renal function permitting); (4) referral to structured education programme (DESMOND); (5) psychology assessment for diabetes distress (PAID score at baseline: 42/80, indicating moderate distress).

Outcomes at 6 Months
HbA1c reduced from 78 to 56 mmol/mol (9.3% to 7.3%), exceeding the target reduction of 11 mmol/mol. Body weight decreased by 8.4 kg (from 108.6 kg to 100.2 kg). eGFR remained stable at 50 mL/min/1.73m2. PAID score improved to 24/80. The patient reported no episodes of hypoglycaemia. Mild nausea was reported during semaglutide titration (weeks 2-6), managed conservatively and self-resolving. Retinopathy status remained stable on 6-month review imaging.

Plan
Continue current regimen. Repeat HbA1c and renal function at 3 months. Annual retinopathy screening. Re-assess cardiovascular risk using QRISK3 at next review. Psychology follow-up at 3 months to consolidate self-management gains.

label: Weak case report with incomplete data and vague outcomes
text:
Case Report: Patient with Knee Pain

A middle-aged patient presented with ongoing knee pain that had been worsening over several months. The patient reported difficulty walking and climbing stairs, along with some intermittent swelling particularly after activity. Past medical history was noted but not detailed here. The patient mentioned having tried over-the-counter remedies with limited success.

Examination revealed tenderness around the knee joint, particularly on the medial aspect. Some limitation of movement was observed, especially on full flexion. An X-ray was requested which showed some changes consistent with wear and tear. Blood tests were also considered but not ordered at this stage.

The patient was started on pain medication and advised to rest and avoid activities that aggravate the symptoms. Physiotherapy was recommended for strengthening exercises. Weight management advice was given as appropriate. A follow-up appointment was arranged for a later date to review progress and assess response to treatment.

The patient's condition may improve with conservative management over the coming weeks. If symptoms do not improve, further investigations such as MRI could be considered. The patient was advised to return sooner if symptoms worsened significantly or if new symptoms developed.

label: Competent assessment following structure and referencing guidelines
text:
Psychiatric Assessment: Moderate Depressive Episode

Presenting Complaint
A 34-year-old female teacher referred by GP with a 4-month history of low mood, anhedonia, early morning wakening, and reduced concentration. PHQ-9 score at referral: 16/27 (moderately severe). No active suicidal ideation, though endorses passive thoughts of 'not wanting to be here' approximately twice weekly. No history of self-harm.

Background
Previous episode of depression aged 26, treated with sertraline 100mg for 12 months with good response. No psychiatric admissions. Family history of depression (mother). No substance misuse. Currently on no psychiatric medication; takes levothyroxine 75mcg for hypothyroidism.

Mental State Examination
Appearance: appropriately dressed, slightly reduced self-care. Behaviour: cooperative, reduced eye contact. Speech: normal rate and volume, reduced spontaneity. Mood: 'flat and grey' (subjective); objectively low. Affect: restricted range, reactive. Thought: no formal disorder; content focused on guilt and inadequacy regarding work performance. Perception: no abnormalities. Cognition: subjective poor concentration, MMSE not formally administered. Insight: good, recognises current state as illness.

Formulation and Plan
This presentation is consistent with a moderate depressive episode (ICD-11: 6A70.1), with identifiable maintaining factors including occupational stress and social withdrawal. In line with NICE CG90 recommendations for moderate depression: (1) commence sertraline 50mg, titrating to 100mg at 2 weeks if tolerated; (2) refer for CBT (high-intensity IAPT); (3) safety plan documented; (4) review in 2 weeks for tolerability and risk assessment; (5) fit note issued for 4 weeks. Consider occupational health referral if not improved by 8 weeks.

label: Template-filled report with correct structure but generic statements
text:
Patient Assessment Report

Patient Information
The patient is an adult who presented to the clinic for routine assessment. Relevant medical history was reviewed and noted. The patient's medication list was checked and reconciled against the clinical record. Allergies were confirmed and documented.

Clinical Findings
A thorough clinical examination was conducted in accordance with standard protocols. Vital signs were within normal limits. The patient's general appearance was appropriate and they appeared comfortable at rest. A full systems review was conducted and findings were documented in the clinical record. No acute concerns were identified on examination.

Investigations
Appropriate investigations were ordered based on the clinical presentation and relevant guidelines. Results were reviewed and considered in the context of the patient's overall clinical picture and baseline values. Any abnormal findings were noted and will be addressed in the management plan. Outstanding results will be reviewed at the next appointment.

Management Plan
A comprehensive management plan was developed in consultation with the patient, taking into account their preferences, clinical needs, and social circumstances. Appropriate medications were prescribed in line with current evidence-based guidelines. Follow-up arrangements were made at an appropriate interval to monitor progress. The patient was advised on warning signs and red flags that should prompt earlier review or emergency attendance. All relevant information was communicated clearly to the patient and documented in the clinical record.

label: Good research summary with clear methodology and statistical results
text:
Research Summary: Efficacy of Structured Exercise Programme in Post-Stroke Upper Limb Rehabilitation

Background
Upper limb impairment affects approximately 70% of stroke survivors and is a primary driver of long-term disability. Current evidence supports intensive, task-specific rehabilitation, but optimal dosing and programme structure remain uncertain. This single-centre pragmatic randomised controlled trial compared a 12-week structured exercise programme against usual care in patients with moderate upper limb impairment (Action Research Arm Test score 20-45) following first ischaemic stroke.

Methods
Consecutive patients (n=94) admitted to the regional stroke unit between March 2024 and February 2025 were screened. Sixty-two met inclusion criteria and were randomised 1:1 to intervention (n=31) or usual care (n=31). The intervention comprised three 60-minute supervised sessions per week for 12 weeks (total dose: 36 hours), incorporating task-specific practice, strength training, and bilateral coordination exercises. The control group received standard physiotherapy (mean 1.8 sessions/week, 45 minutes each). The primary outcome was change in ARAT score at 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes included grip strength (Jamar dynamometer), patient-reported upper limb function (DASH questionnaire), and adverse events. Assessors were blinded to group allocation.

Results
Fifty-seven participants completed the 12-week assessment (attrition: 8.1%, balanced across groups). The intervention group showed a mean ARAT improvement of 12.4 points (SD 6.8) compared to 7.1 points (SD 7.2) in the control group (adjusted mean difference: 5.3 points, 95% CI: 2.1 to 8.5, p=0.002). This exceeds the minimal clinically important difference of 5.7 points. Grip strength improved by 4.2 kg in the intervention group versus 1.8 kg in controls (p=0.01). DASH scores improved in both groups with no statistically significant between-group difference (p=0.09). Two adverse events were recorded in the intervention group (one shoulder pain requiring session modification, one unrelated fall), and one in the control group.

Conclusions
A structured, higher-dose exercise programme produced clinically and statistically significant improvements in upper limb motor function compared to usual care. The intervention was feasible and well-tolerated, with low attrition and minimal adverse events. Limitations include the single-centre design, moderate sample size, and lack of longer-term follow-up. A multicentre trial with 6-month and 12-month outcomes is warranted. These findings support current Stroke Association recommendations for increased rehabilitation intensity where resources permit.
